title: Note Template & Blueprint
text: Welcome to the Note Template & Blueprint. This document serves as a layout and UI reference for creating new notes. By copying this structure, note creators ensure that definitions, equations, diagrams, interactive components, and problems render in a unified, premium dark theme.

title: Note Template & Blueprint
text:
1. Definitions
Every note should begin with a precise, mathematically rigorous definition of the central concept. Wrap definitions in the .thm-box .thm-definition fenced div container.
To see how this mathematical formulation is applied in a concrete scenario, scroll down to Practice Problem 1.

Definition: [Concept Name]
For a system or variable state, the [Concept Name] is defined as…
Let \(X\) be a random variable, then the mathematical formulation is: \[
f(X) = \mathcal{G}(X) \cdot \lambda
\] where: * \(\mathcal{G}(X)\) represents the system gain operator. * \(\lambda\) is the normalization constant.

title: Note Template & Blueprint
text:
2. Intuitions & Remarks
To make the note highly pedagogical, follow the definition with a real-world intuition or remark. Wrap these insights in the .thm-box .thm-remark block.

Physical Intuition
Think of this concept like… [Insert a clear analogy here]. For example, if you are looking at a search space: > “High system gain means searching broadly, whereas a low system gain means narrowing down the search parameters.”

title: Note Template & Blueprint
text:
3. Formulas & Key Properties
Present the main derivations, properties, and proofs in this section. Group related formulas and characteristics inside .thm-box .thm-theorem blocks to make them pop visually.

Theorem: Additivity and Non-Negativity
Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be independent entities. The system satisfies the following foundational axioms:

1. Non-negativity
For any valid state \(x\): \[
f(x) \ge 0
\]


2. Joint Additivity
For joint independent states: \[
f(x, y) = f(x) + f(y)
\]

title: Note Template & Blueprint
text:
7. Practice Problems with Collapsible Solutions
Conclude each note with 1–3 concrete practice problems. Readers can click anchor links to jump directly here. Wrap solutions inside <details class="solution-toggle"> elements so they remain collapsed by default.

Problem 1: Basic Derivation
Show that if two events are independent, their joint surprisal adds up.
(This problem directly tests your understanding of the concepts defined in the Definitions section.)


Reveal Solution to Problem 1




Solution

Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be independent random variables. By definition: \[
P(X, Y) = P(X)P(Y)
\] Taking the negative log base 2 of both sides: \[
\begin{aligned}
h(X, Y) &= -\log_2 P(X, Y) \\
&= -\log_2 \big(P(X)P(Y)\big) \\
&= -\log_2 P(X) - \log_2 P(Y) \\
&= h(X) + h(Y)
\end{aligned}
\] Hence, joint surprisal is additive. \(\blacksquare\)





Problem 2: Applied Metric
Explain why Cross-Entropy is always greater than or equal to Entropy: \(H(P, Q) \ge H(P)\).
(This problem requires applying the mathematical equivalence detailed in the Relations to Other Fields section.)


Reveal Solution to Problem 2




Solution

Recall that the Kullback-Leibler (KL) Divergence represents: \[
D_{\text{KL}}(P \parallel Q) = H(P, Q) - H(P)
\] By Gibbs’ Inequality, we know that \(D_{\text{KL}}(P \parallel Q) \ge 0\) for all probability distributions \(P\) and \(Q\). Consequently: \[
H(P, Q) - H(P) \ge 0 \implies H(P, Q) \ge H(P)
\] The inequality holds with equality if and only if \(P = Q\). \(\blacksquare\)

title: Stochastic Matrices
text:
A matrix \(P \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times N}\) is called a stochastic matrix (or Markov matrix) if: 1. All entries are non-negative: \([P]_{ij} \ge 0\). 2. It is row stochastic: Each row sums to 1 (\(\sum_{j=0}^{N-1} [P]_{ij} = 1\)).
If the matrix also satisfies that every column sums to 1, it is called a doubly stochastic matrix.

A stationary distribution \(\boldsymbol\pi\) (represented as a column vector) for a Markov chain with transition matrix \(P\) satisfies: \[
\boldsymbol\pi^\top P = \boldsymbol\pi^\top
\] meaning it is the left eigenvector corresponding to the eigenvalue \(\lambda = 1\).

title: Stochastic Matrices
text:
A matrix \(P \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times N}\) is called a stochastic matrix (or Markov matrix) if: 1. All entries are non-negative: \([P]_{ij} \ge 0\). 2. It is row stochastic: Each row sums to 1 (\(\sum_{j=0}^{N-1} [P]_{ij} = 1\)).
If the matrix also satisfies that every column sums to 1, it is called a doubly stochastic matrix.

A stationary distribution \(\boldsymbol\pi\) (represented as a column vector) for a Markov chain with transition matrix \(P\) satisfies: \[
\boldsymbol\pi^\top P = \boldsymbol\pi^\top
\] meaning it is the left eigenvector corresponding to the eigenvalue \(\lambda = 1\).

title: Stochastic Matrices
text:
Intuition
A stochastic matrix encodes all the probabilistic transitions between states in a Markov chain. The rows represent the current state, and the columns represent the next state. Because a state must transition somewhere, the probabilities across the row must sum to 1.
Important properties: - The dominant eigenvalue is exactly 1. - Repeatedly multiplying a probability vector by the transition matrix simulates moving forward in time. Over a long time period (\(P^k\) as \(k \to \infty\)), an ergodic Markov chain converges to its stationary distribution, meaning the long-term proportion of time spent in each state stabilizes. - Doubly stochastic matrices uniquely have a uniform stationary distribution. The Birkhoff-von Neumann theorem states they can be expressed as convex combinations of permutation matrices. Algorithms like Sinkhorn-Knopp are used to convert non-negative matrices into doubly stochastic matrices.

title: Stochastic Matrices
text:
Practice Problems

1. Stationary Distribution
Given a stochastic matrix, set up the linear system to solve for the stationary distribution \(\boldsymbol\pi^\top P = \boldsymbol\pi^\top\) with the constraint \(\sum \pi_i = 1\).


2. Matrix Powers
Prove that if \(P\) is a stochastic matrix, then the power \(P^k\) is also a stochastic matrix for any positive integer \(k\).


3. Doubly Stochastic Stationary Distribution
For an ergodic doubly stochastic matrix \(P\) of size \(N \times N\), prove that the stationary distribution is uniform: \(\boldsymbol\pi = \frac{1}{N} \mathbf{1}_N\).

title: Evaluation Metrics for Ranking and IR
text:
For a ranking or information retrieval (IR) system, evaluating the top \(K\) retrieved items is standard:
Precision@K (P@K): The proportion of relevant items among the top \(K\) retrieved items: \[
\text{P@K} = \frac{\text{Number of relevant items in top } K}{K}
\]
Recall@K (R@K): The proportion of all relevant items that appear in the top \(K\): \[
\text{R@K} = \frac{\text{Number of relevant items in top } K}{\text{Total number of relevant items}}
\]



Average Precision (AP) for a single query is the average of precision values computed at each position \(k\) where a relevant item is found: \[
\text{AP} = \frac{1}{|\text{Relevant}|} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \text{Precision@}k \cdot \mathbf{1}\{\text{item at position } k \text{ is relevant}\}
\]
Mean Average Precision (MAP) is the mean of Average Precision scores across \(Q\) queries: \[
\text{MAP} = \frac{1}{Q} \sum_{q=1}^{Q} \text{AP}_q
\]

title: Evaluation Metrics for Ranking and IR
text:
For a ranking or information retrieval (IR) system, evaluating the top \(K\) retrieved items is standard:
Precision@K (P@K): The proportion of relevant items among the top \(K\) retrieved items: \[
\text{P@K} = \frac{\text{Number of relevant items in top } K}{K}
\]
Recall@K (R@K): The proportion of all relevant items that appear in the top \(K\): \[
\text{R@K} = \frac{\text{Number of relevant items in top } K}{\text{Total number of relevant items}}
\]



Average Precision (AP) for a single query is the average of precision values computed at each position \(k\) where a relevant item is found: \[
\text{AP} = \frac{1}{|\text{Relevant}|} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \text{Precision@}k \cdot \mathbf{1}\{\text{item at position } k \text{ is relevant}\}
\]
Mean Average Precision (MAP) is the mean of Average Precision scores across \(Q\) queries: \[
\text{MAP} = \frac{1}{Q} \sum_{q=1}^{Q} \text{AP}_q
\]

title: Evaluation Metrics for Ranking and IR
text:
Intuition
While precision and recall are useful, they do not care about order. A system that puts the relevant result at position #1 has the same Precision@10 as a system that puts it at position #10.
MAP naturally solves this. By averaging the precision at every point a relevant item is found, it heavily rewards systems that rank the relevant items near the very top. MAP balances both precision and recall while simultaneously taking the ranking order into account, making it the gold standard metric for search engines, recommendation systems, and object detection.

title: Evaluation Metrics for Ranking and IR
text:
Practice Problems

1. Calculating Precision/Recall
A search engine retrieves 8 documents for a query. The relevance labels (1 = relevant, 0 = irrelevant) in order are: \([1, 1, 0, 1, 0, 0, 1, 0]\). Compute Precision@3, Precision@5, Recall@3, and Recall@5.


2. Calculating AP
Using the same sequence \([1, 1, 0, 1, 0, 0, 1, 0]\) and assuming there are exactly 4 relevant documents total in the entire corpus, calculate the Average Precision (AP).


3. Metric Comparison
In information retrieval, explain why MAP is preferred over simple precision or recall for evaluating ranking systems. Give a theoretical example where two systems have the exact same precision and recall but vastly different MAP values.

title: Mutual Information
text:
Mutual Information (MI) quantifies the amount of information obtained about one random variable by observing another random variable. Intuitively, it measures how much knowing \(Y\) reduces the uncertainty about \(X\).
If \(X\) and \(Y\) are completely independent, knowing \(Y\) tells you absolutely nothing about \(X\), and their mutual information is zero. If \(X\) is a deterministic function of \(Y\), knowing \(Y\) tells you everything about \(X\), and their mutual information is maximized.
Unlike correlation, which only captures linear dependencies, Mutual Information captures all non-linear dependencies between variables.

title: Mutual Information
text:
Practice Problems

Problem 1: Independence
If two random variables \(X\) and \(Y\) are statistically independent, prove directly from the summation definition that their Mutual Information \(I(X;Y)\) is exactly zero.

title: Kullback-Leibler Divergence
text:
For discrete probability distributions \(P\) and \(Q\) defined on the same probability space \(\mathcal{X}\), the Kullback-Leibler (KL) Divergence from \(Q\) to \(P\) is defined as:
\[
D_{KL}(P \| Q) = \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} P(x) \log_2 \left( \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)} \right)
\]
For continuous distributions with probability density functions \(p\) and \(q\), the sum becomes an integral: \[
D_{KL}(P \| Q) = \int_{\mathcal{X}} p(x) \ln \left( \frac{p(x)}{q(x)} \right) dx
\]
(Note: The double vertical bars \(\|\) emphasize that this is a divergence between two distributions, not a conditional probability. It is read as “The KL divergence of P with respect to Q” or “The KL divergence from Q to P”.)

title: Kullback-Leibler Divergence
text:
For discrete probability distributions \(P\) and \(Q\) defined on the same probability space \(\mathcal{X}\), the Kullback-Leibler (KL) Divergence from \(Q\) to \(P\) is defined as:
\[
D_{KL}(P \| Q) = \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} P(x) \log_2 \left( \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)} \right)
\]
For continuous distributions with probability density functions \(p\) and \(q\), the sum becomes an integral: \[
D_{KL}(P \| Q) = \int_{\mathcal{X}} p(x) \ln \left( \frac{p(x)}{q(x)} \right) dx
\]
(Note: The double vertical bars \(\|\) emphasize that this is a divergence between two distributions, not a conditional probability. It is read as “The KL divergence of P with respect to Q” or “The KL divergence from Q to P”.)

title: Kullback-Leibler Divergence
text:
2. Intuitions & Remarks

The Intuition of Information Loss
If \(P\) represents the “true” underlying distribution of data in nature, and \(Q\) represents our mathematical model’s approximation of that distribution, then \(D_{KL}(P \| Q)\) quantifies the exact amount of information lost when we use \(Q\) instead of \(P\).
Imagine you are trying to compress data using a codebook. If you know the true distribution \(P\), you can build an optimal codebook. If you are forced to use a codebook optimized for \(Q\) instead, your compressed files will be slightly larger. The KL divergence is exactly the number of extra bits required due to using the wrong codebook.

title: Kullback-Leibler Divergence
text:
3. Formulas & Key Properties

Key Properties of KL Divergence

1. Gibbs’ Inequality (Non-negativity)
KL Divergence is strictly non-negative. You cannot have “negative” information loss. \[
D_{KL}(P \| Q) \ge 0
\] Equality \(D_{KL}(P \| Q) = 0\) holds if and only if \(P\) and \(Q\) are identical almost everywhere.


2. Lack of Symmetry
KL Divergence is not a true mathematical distance metric because it is highly asymmetric: \[
D_{KL}(P \| Q) \neq D_{KL}(Q \| P)
\] This asymmetry has profound implications in machine learning, dictating whether models exhibit “mode-seeking” or “mean-seeking” behavior.


3. Lack of Triangle Inequality
It does not satisfy the triangle inequality. You cannot compute the divergence \(D_{KL}(P \| R)\) simply by bridging through an intermediate distribution \(Q\). Thus, we call it a “divergence” rather than a “distance”.

title: Kullback-Leibler Divergence
text:
4. Graphs & Visualizations
Because of the asymmetry, the order of \(P\) and \(Q\) drastically alters the geometric behavior of the approximation when we use KL Divergence as an optimization objective.

Forward KL: \(D_{KL}(P \| Q)\)

Penalty: If \(P(x) > 0\) but \(Q(x) \to 0\), the ratio \(\frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}\) explodes to infinity. Therefore, \(Q\) is forced to place probability mass everywhere \(P\) places mass.
Result: \(Q\) stretches out to “cover” all the modes (peaks) of \(P\), resulting in a broad, mean-seeking approximation.



Reverse KL: \(D_{KL}(Q \| P)\)

Penalty: If \(Q(x) > 0\) but \(P(x) \to 0\), the ratio \(\frac{Q(x)}{P(x)}\) explodes. Therefore, \(Q\) must absolutely avoid placing mass where \(P\) has no mass.
Result: \(Q\) tends to “collapse” into a single valid peak (mode) of \(P\) to safely avoid areas where \(P=0\). This results in a highly focused, mode-seeking approximation.

title: Kullback-Leibler Divergence
text:
7. Practice Problems

Problem 1: Proof of Asymmetry
Provide a concrete, numerical proof that \(D_{KL}(P \| Q) \neq D_{KL}(Q \| P)\) using two simple discrete probability distributions: \(P=[0.5, 0.5]\) and \(Q=[0.9, 0.1]\).


Reveal Solution to Problem 1




Solution

Let \(P = [0.5, 0.5]\) and \(Q = [0.9, 0.1]\).
Forward KL Divergence: \[
\begin{aligned}
D_{KL}(P \| Q) &= \sum P(x) \log_2 \left( \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)} \right) \\
&= 0.5 \log_2\left(\frac{0.5}{0.9}\right) + 0.5 \log_2\left(\frac{0.5}{0.1}\right) \\
&= 0.5 \log_2(0.555) + 0.5 \log_2(5) \\
&\approx 0.5(-0.849) + 0.5(2.321) \\
&\approx -0.424 + 1.161 \\
&= 0.737 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
\]
Reverse KL Divergence: \[
\begin{aligned}
D_{KL}(Q \| P) &= \sum Q(x) \log_2 \left( \frac{Q(x)}{P(x)} \right) \\
&= 0.9 \log_2\left(\frac{0.9}{0.5}\right) + 0.1 \log_2\left(\frac{0.1}{0.5}\right) \\
&= 0.9 \log_2(1.8) + 0.1 \log_2(0.2) \\
&\approx 0.9(0.848) + 0.1(-2.321) \\
&\approx 0.763 - 0.232 \\
&= 0.531 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
\]
Since \(0.737 \neq 0.531\), we have proven that the KL divergence is fundamentally asymmetric. \(\blacksquare\)





Problem 2: Gibbs’ Inequality
Use Jensen’s Inequality to mathematically prove Gibbs’ Inequality: \(D_{KL}(P \| Q) \ge 0\). (Hint: Remember that Jensen’s Inequality states that for any strictly concave function like \(\ln(x)\), the expected value of the function is less than or equal to the function of the expected value: \(\mathbb{E}[\ln(X)] \le \ln(\mathbb{E}[X])\)).


Reveal Solution to Problem 2




Solution

Let’s begin with the definition of KL Divergence using the natural logarithm: \[
D_{KL}(P \| Q) = \sum_{x} P(x) \ln \left( \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)} \right)
\]
We can rewrite the ratio inside the logarithm by negating it: \[
D_{KL}(P \| Q) = -\sum_{x} P(x) \ln \left( \frac{Q(x)}{P(x)} \right)
\]
Notice that \(\sum_{x} P(x) [\dots]\) is exactly the definition of the mathematical expectation \(\mathbb{E}_{X \sim P}[\dots]\). Thus: \[
D_{KL}(P \| Q) = -\mathbb{E}_{X \sim P} \left[ \ln \left( \frac{Q(X)}{P(X)} \right) \right]
\]
Since the natural logarithm function \(\ln(z)\) is strictly concave, we can apply Jensen’s Inequality, which states that \(-\mathbb{E}[\ln(Z)] \ge -\ln(\mathbb{E}[Z])\): \[
-\mathbb{E}_{X \sim P} \left[ \ln \left( \frac{Q(X)}{P(X)} \right) \right] \ge -\ln \left( \mathbb{E}_{X \sim P} \left[ \frac{Q(X)}{P(X)} \right] \right)
\]
Now, let’s evaluate the inner expectation: \[
\mathbb{E}_{X \sim P} \left[ \frac{Q(X)}{P(X)} \right] = \sum_{x} P(x) \frac{Q(x)}{P(x)} = \sum_{x} Q(x) = 1
\] Because \(Q\) is a valid probability distribution, its probabilities must sum to 1.
Substituting this back into the inequality: \[
D_{KL}(P \| Q) \ge -\ln(1) = 0
\]
Therefore, \(D_{KL}(P \| Q) \ge 0\). \(\blacksquare\)

title: Information Content (Surprisal)
text:
Every concept in Information Theory stems from quantifying how much information we gain when an event occurs.


For a discrete random variable \(X\) with a probability mass function \(P(X)\), the information content (or surprisal) of a specific event \(x\) is defined as the negative logarithm of its probability:
\[
h(x) = -\log_b P(x) = \log_b \left( \frac{1}{P(x)} \right)
\]
where:

\(P(x)\) is the prior probability of the event occurring.
\(b\) is the base of the logarithm, which determines the unit of information:

Base 2 (\(b=2\)): Measured in bits (or shannons). This is the standard in computer science. One bit represents the information gained from a single fair coin flip.
Base \(e\) (\(b=e\)): Measured in nats. Commonly used in machine learning because the natural logarithm is easier to differentiate.
Base 10 (\(b=10\)): Measured in bans (or hartleys), though this is rarely used in modern contexts.

title: Information Content (Surprisal)
text:
2. Intuitions & Remarks

The Intuition of Surprise
The core intuition relies on a simple premise: > Observing a highly probable event provides very little information, whereas observing a rare or highly improbable event provides a massive amount of information.
If someone tells you, “The sun rose this morning,” you have gained almost zero information because the probability of that event is near \(1\). Conversely, if someone tells you, “It is currently snowing in the Sahara Desert,” you have gained a tremendous amount of information because the prior probability of that event is near \(0\).

title: Information Content (Surprisal)
text:
3. Formulas & Key Properties
The definition \(h(x) = -\log P(x)\) was specifically chosen by Claude Shannon because it perfectly satisfies three necessary axioms of information:

Axioms of Information

1. Non-negativity
Information cannot be negative. You cannot “lose” information by observing an event. \[
P(x) \in [0, 1] \implies h(x) \ge 0
\]


2. Zero Surprise for Certainty
If an event is guaranteed to happen (\(P(x) = 1\)), observing it yields no new information. \[
h(x) = -\log(1) = 0
\]


3. Joint Additivity
If two events \(x\) and \(y\) are statistically independent, the joint probability is the product of their individual probabilities. Consequently, the total information gained from observing both events is the sum of the information gained from observing them individually.
To see the exact mathematical derivation of this property, scroll down to Practice Problem 1.

title: Information Content (Surprisal)
text:
7. Practice Problems

Problem 1: Additivity of Independent Events
Show mathematically that if two events are statistically independent, their joint surprisal adds up (\(h(X, Y) = h(X) + h(Y)\)).


Reveal Solution to Problem 1




Solution

Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be independent random variables. By the definition of independence, their joint probability is the product of their marginals: \[
P(X, Y) = P(X)P(Y)
\] Taking the negative log base 2 of both sides: \[
\begin{aligned}
h(X, Y) &= -\log_2 P(X, Y) \\
&= -\log_2 \big(P(X)P(Y)\big) \\
&= -\log_2 P(X) - \log_2 P(Y) \\
&= h(X) + h(Y)
\end{aligned}
\] Hence, joint surprisal is additive. \(\blacksquare\)





Problem 2: Calculating Surprisal
Calculate the surprisal of rolling a 6 on a fair 6-sided die, in both bits and nats. How does this compare to the surprisal of flipping two heads in a row on a fair coin?


Reveal Solution to Problem 2




Solution

Die Roll: The probability of rolling a 6 is \(P(x) = \frac{1}{6}\). In bits (base 2): \[
h(x) = -\log_2\left(\frac{1}{6}\right) = \log_2(6) \approx 2.585 \text{ bits}
\] In nats (base \(e\)): \[
h(x) = -\ln\left(\frac{1}{6}\right) = \ln(6) \approx 1.792 \text{ nats}
\]
Coin Flips: The probability of flipping two heads in a row is \(P(y) = \frac{1}{4}\). In bits: \[
h(y) = -\log_2\left(\frac{1}{4}\right) = \log_2(4) = 2 \text{ bits}
\]
Rolling a 6 provides more surprise (2.585 bits) than flipping two heads in a row (2 bits) because \(\frac{1}{6}\) is less probable than \(\frac{1}{4}\).

title: Fisher Information
text:
In classical Information Theory (Shannon Entropy), we measure the information inherent in the data itself. In statistical inference, however, we often care about Fisher Information: a measure of the amount of information that an observable random variable \(X\) carries about an unknown, unobservable parameter \(\theta\).
If the probability distribution \(p(x | \theta)\) changes rapidly when \(\theta\) changes slightly, then observing \(X\) gives us a massive amount of information about the true value of \(\theta\). If \(p(x | \theta)\) is flat and barely reacts to changes in \(\theta\), observing \(X\) tells us almost nothing.

title: Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)
text:
In probabilistic machine learning, we often posit that our observable data \(x\) is generated by some hidden, unobservable latent variables \(z\).
To perform inference, we need to calculate the posterior probability of the latents given the data, using Bayes’ Theorem:
\[
p(z | x) = \frac{p(x | z) p(z)}{p(x)}
\]
The denominator \(p(x)\) is called the Evidence. To compute it, we must marginalize (integrate) over every possible configuration of the latent variables \(z\):
\[
p(x) = \int p(x | z) p(z) \, dz
\]
For modern deep learning models, \(z\) is continuous and high-dimensional. This integral is mathematically intractable to compute. If we can’t compute the evidence \(p(x)\), we can’t compute the posterior \(p(z | x)\), and we cannot train our model.

title: Entropy
text:
While Information Content (Surprisal) measures the information gained from a single specific event, Entropy measures the average amount of information produced by a stochastic source of data.


For a discrete random variable \(X\) with possible states \(x_1, \dots, x_n\) and probability mass function \(P(x)\), the Shannon Entropy \(H(X)\) is defined as the mathematical expectation of the surprisal \(h(X)\):
\[
H(X) = \mathbb{E}_{X \sim P}[h(X)] = -\sum_{x \in X} P(x) \log_2 P(x)
\]
(Note: In the case where \(P(x) = 0\), we adopt the convention that \(0 \log_2 0 = 0\), which is consistent with the limit \(\lim_{p \to 0} p \log_2 p = 0\).)

title: Entropy
text:
While Information Content (Surprisal) measures the information gained from a single specific event, Entropy measures the average amount of information produced by a stochastic source of data.


For a discrete random variable \(X\) with possible states \(x_1, \dots, x_n\) and probability mass function \(P(x)\), the Shannon Entropy \(H(X)\) is defined as the mathematical expectation of the surprisal \(h(X)\):
\[
H(X) = \mathbb{E}_{X \sim P}[h(X)] = -\sum_{x \in X} P(x) \log_2 P(x)
\]
(Note: In the case where \(P(x) = 0\), we adopt the convention that \(0 \log_2 0 = 0\), which is consistent with the limit \(\lim_{p \to 0} p \log_2 p = 0\).)

title: Entropy
text:
2. Intuitions & Remarks

Expected Surprisal
Because Entropy is the Expected Information Content, it gives us a single scalar value describing the “flatness” or “chaos” of a distribution.

Low Entropy: The distribution is highly skewed or deterministic. The outcomes are predictable. Because you almost always know what is going to happen, observing an outcome yields very little new information on average.
High Entropy: The distribution is flat and uniform. The outcomes are highly unpredictable. Because you never know what is going to happen, each observation provides a lot of new information.

Imagine a heavily biased coin that lands on Heads 99% of the time. The entropy is extremely low because you are rarely surprised. But a fair coin (50/50) has maximum entropy; the outcome is a complete mystery every time.

title: Entropy
text:
3. Formulas & Key Properties

Key Properties of Entropy

1. Bounds
For a discrete variable with \(k\) possible states, the entropy is strictly bounded: \[
0 \le H(X) \le \log_2 k
\] * \(H(X) = 0\) if and only if the outcome is completely certain (i.e., \(P(x_i) = 1\) for some \(i\) and \(0\) for all others). * \(H(X) = \log_2 k\) if and only if \(X\) follows a Uniform Distribution (\(P(x_i) = \frac{1}{k}\) for all \(i\)). The uniform distribution represents the state of maximum uncertainty.


2. Concavity
Entropy \(H(P)\) is a strictly concave function of the probability distribution \(P\). This mathematically formalizes the idea that mixing two different probability distributions together increases the overall uncertainty.


3. Continuous Extension (Differential Entropy)
For a continuous random variable \(X\) with a probability density function \(p(x)\), the sum is replaced by an integral: \[
h(X) = -\int_{\mathcal{X}} p(x) \ln p(x) \, dx
\] Warning: Unlike discrete entropy, differential entropy can be negative, and it is not invariant to reparameterization.

title: Entropy
text:
7. Practice Problems

Problem 1: Biased Coin Entropy
Prove that the entropy of a fair coin flip is exactly 1 bit. What happens to the theoretical entropy \(H(X)\) if the coin is heavily biased such that \(P(\text{Heads}) = 0.99\)?


Reveal Solution to Problem 1




Solution

Fair Coin: For a fair coin, \(P(\text{Heads}) = 0.5\) and \(P(\text{Tails}) = 0.5\). Using the Shannon Entropy formula: \[
\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= - \sum_{x} P(x) \log_2 P(x) \\
&= - [0.5 \log_2(0.5) + 0.5 \log_2(0.5)] \\
&= - [0.5(-1) + 0.5(-1)] \\
&= 1 \text{ bit}
\end{aligned}
\]
Biased Coin: For a biased coin with \(P(\text{Heads}) = 0.99\) and \(P(\text{Tails}) = 0.01\): \[
\begin{aligned}
H(X) &= - [0.99 \log_2(0.99) + 0.01 \log_2(0.01)] \\
&\approx - [0.99(-0.0145) + 0.01(-6.6438)] \\
&\approx 0.0143 + 0.0664 \\
&\approx 0.0807 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
\]
The entropy drops from 1 bit to roughly 0.08 bits. Because the outcome is almost certain, you gain very little information on average from observing the coin flips.





Problem 2: Maximum Entropy of a Uniform Distribution
Use the method of Lagrange multipliers to prove that for a discrete random variable \(X\) with \(k\) possible states, the entropy \(H(X)\) is maximized when the distribution is uniform, i.e., \(P(x_i) = \frac{1}{k}\) for all \(i\).


Reveal Solution to Problem 2




Solution

We want to maximize the entropy function: \[
H(P) = -\sum_{i=1}^k p_i \ln p_i
\] (Note: We use the natural logarithm \(\ln\) to simplify differentiation, which only scales the result by a constant and does not change the argmax).
This maximization is subject to the probability constraint that the sum of all probabilities must equal 1: \[
\sum_{i=1}^k p_i = 1 \implies \sum_{i=1}^k p_i - 1 = 0
\]
We define the Lagrangian \(\mathcal{L}\) with a multiplier \(\lambda\): \[
\mathcal{L}(p_1, \dots, p_k, \lambda) = -\sum_{i=1}^k p_i \ln p_i + \lambda \left(\sum_{i=1}^k p_i - 1\right)
\]
Take the partial derivative with respect to a specific \(p_i\) and set it to 0: \[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial p_i} &= -(\ln p_i + p_i \cdot \frac{1}{p_i}) + \lambda = 0 \\
&= -\ln p_i - 1 + \lambda = 0
\end{aligned}
\]
Solving for \(p_i\): \[
\ln p_i = \lambda - 1 \implies p_i = e^{\lambda - 1}
\]
Notice that the solution for \(p_i\) depends only on \(\lambda\), which is a constant. This means every \(p_i\) must be exactly the same! Since there are \(k\) states and they must sum to 1: \[
p_1 = p_2 = \dots = p_k = \frac{1}{k}
\]
Thus, the Uniform Distribution maximizes entropy. \(\blacksquare\)

title: Lemon's Website
text:
Problem 1: The Exponential Distribution
For a continuous random variable \(X\) distributed exponentially with a rate parameter \(\lambda > 0\), the probability density function is \(f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x}\) for \(x \ge 0\). Part A: Calculate its differential entropy \(h(X)\) in nats. Part B: Conceptually, what happens to the differential entropy as the rate \(\lambda\) becomes very large? Why does this make physical sense?
Problem 2: The Gaussian (Normal) Distribution
For a continuous random variable \(X\) with a normal distribution, the probability density function is \(f(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}\) for \(-\infty < x < \infty\). Part A: Calculate its differential entropy \(h(X)\) in nats. (Hint: You do not need to do complex integration for the second term if you recognize it as the expected value or variance!) Part B: Does the mean (\(\mu\)) affect the entropy of the distribution? Why or why not?
Problem 3: The Linear Sloped Distribution
Consider a continuous random variable \(X\) with a probability density function \(f(x) = 2x\) on the interval \([0, 1]\). Part A: Calculate its differential entropy \(h(X)\) in nats. (Hint: You will need to use Integration by Parts: \(\int u \, dv = uv - \int v \, du\).) Part B: The uniform distribution on the interval \([0, 1]\) has an entropy of exactly \(0\). Is the entropy of this linear distribution higher or lower than \(0\)? Geometrically, why must this be the case?
Problem 4: The Laplace Distribution
For a continuous random variable \(X\) following a Laplace distribution with scale parameter \(b > 0\) centered at \(0\), the probability density function is \(f(x) = \frac{1}{2b} e^{-\frac{|x|}{b}}\) for \(-\infty < x < \infty\). Part A: Calculate its differential entropy \(h(X)\) in nats. (Hint: Because the function is perfectly symmetric, you can just integrate from \(0\) to \(\infty\) and multiply by 2). Part B: Compare your answer to the Uniform distribution’s entropy (\(\ln(w)\)). If a Laplace distribution and a Uniform distribution have the exact same entropy, how does the scale \(b\) relate to the width \(w\)?
Problem 5: The Scaling Property
Suppose you have a continuous random variable \(X\) with a known probability density function \(f_X(x)\) and a known differential entropy \(h(X)\). You create a new variable \(Y = cX\), where \(c\) is a positive constant. The probability density function of \(Y\) is \(f_Y(y) = \frac{1}{c} f_X\left(\frac{y}{c}\right)\). Part A: Prove that the differential entropy of the new variable is \(h(Y) = h(X) + \ln(c)\). Part B: What happens to the entropy if you multiply your data by a constant \(c\) that is less than 1?

title: Cross-Entropy
text:
For discrete distributions \(P\) and \(Q\) defined over the same sample space \(\mathcal{X}\), the cross-entropy \(H(P, Q)\) is defined as the expected surprisal of the estimated distribution \(Q\), evaluated under the true distribution \(P\):
\[
H(P, Q) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim P} [ -\log_2 Q(x) ] = -\sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} P(x) \log_2 Q(x)
\]
For continuous distributions with probability density functions \(p\) and \(q\), the sum is replaced by an integral: \[
H(P, Q) = -\int_{\mathcal{X}} p(x) \ln q(x) \, dx
\]

title: Cross-Entropy
text:
For discrete distributions \(P\) and \(Q\) defined over the same sample space \(\mathcal{X}\), the cross-entropy \(H(P, Q)\) is defined as the expected surprisal of the estimated distribution \(Q\), evaluated under the true distribution \(P\):
\[
H(P, Q) = \mathbb{E}_{x \sim P} [ -\log_2 Q(x) ] = -\sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} P(x) \log_2 Q(x)
\]
For continuous distributions with probability density functions \(p\) and \(q\), the sum is replaced by an integral: \[
H(P, Q) = -\int_{\mathcal{X}} p(x) \ln q(x) \, dx
\]

title: Cross-Entropy
text:
2. Intuitions & Remarks

The Cost of Being Wrong
In information theory, Cross-Entropy measures the average number of bits needed to identify an event drawn from a true probability distribution \(P\), if a coding scheme is used that is optimized for an estimated (or unnatural) probability distribution \(Q\), rather than the true distribution \(P\).
Intuitively, if you believe the weather follows distribution \(Q\) (e.g., mostly sunny), you will design a communication protocol that assigns very short codes to the message “sunny”. If the actual weather follows distribution \(P\) (e.g., mostly rainy), your protocol will be highly inefficient because you will frequently have to transmit the long, complex codes for “rainy”.
Cross-entropy quantifies this exact inefficiency. It asks: “How many bits on average do I have to transmit if I optimize for \(Q\), but reality actually follows \(P\)?”

title: Cross-Entropy
text:
3. Formulas & Key Properties

Key Properties of Cross-Entropy

1. Non-Symmetric
Cross-entropy is asymmetric. The cost of assuming \(Q\) when reality is \(P\) is completely different from assuming \(P\) when reality is \(Q\). \[
H(P, Q) \neq H(Q, P)
\]


2. Lower Bound (Gibbs’ Inequality)
Cross-entropy is strictly bounded below by the true entropy of the ground truth distribution \(P\). \[
H(P, Q) \ge H(P)
\] Equality holds if and only if your estimation perfectly matches reality (\(P = Q\)).


3. Relationship to KL Divergence
Cross-entropy is deeply connected to Shannon Entropy and the Kullback-Leibler (KL) Divergence via a profound decomposition: \[
H(P, Q) = H(P) + D_{KL}(P \| Q)
\] This equation states that the total cost of using the wrong distribution \(Q\) is exactly equal to the irreducible, theoretical minimum cost if you knew the true distribution \(P\) (which is \(H(P)\)), plus a “penalty gap” for your estimation error (which is the KL Divergence).

Machine Learning Context: Cross-Entropy Loss
When training a neural network classifier, we are trying to predict the true label distribution \(P\) using the model’s predicted distribution \(Q\). - The true distribution \(P\) for a single sample is typically a one-hot encoded vector (e.g., \([0, 1, 0, 0]\) where the target class has probability 1). - The predicted distribution \(Q\) is the output of the Softmax function (e.g., \([0.1, 0.7, 0.15, 0.05]\)).
If we minimize the Cross-Entropy \(H(P, Q)\), we push \(Q\) to be as close to \(P\) as possible.

Why is \(H(P)\) ignored in practice?
Recall the decomposition \(H(P, Q) = H(P) + D_{KL}(P \| Q)\). In a classification task, the true labels \(P\) are deterministic (one-hot), meaning the true entropy \(H(P)\) is exactly \(0\). Therefore, minimizing Cross-Entropy is mathematically identical to minimizing the KL Divergence between the predictions and the ground truth!

title: Cross-Entropy
text:
7. Practice Problems

Problem 1: Decomposition of Cross-Entropy
Mathematically derive the decomposition \(H(P, Q) = H(P) + D_{KL}(P \| Q)\) from the base definition of Cross-Entropy. (Hint: Use the definition of KL Divergence \(D_{KL}(P \| Q) = \sum P(x) \log \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}\)).


Reveal Solution to Problem 1




Solution

Starting from the definition of KL Divergence: \[
\begin{aligned}
D_{KL}(P \| Q) &= \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} P(x) \log \left( \frac{P(x)}{Q(x)} \right) \\
&= \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} P(x) \big( \log P(x) - \log Q(x) \big) \\
&= \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} P(x) \log P(x) - \sum_{x \in \mathcal{X}} P(x) \log Q(x)
\end{aligned}
\]
Notice that the first term is exactly the negative entropy of \(P\), and the second term is the positive Cross-Entropy \(H(P, Q)\): \[
D_{KL}(P \| Q) = -H(P) + H(P, Q)
\]
Rearranging the terms, we get the fundamental relationship: \[
H(P, Q) = H(P) + D_{KL}(P \| Q)
\]
\(\blacksquare\)





Problem 2: Binary Cross-Entropy Penalty Explosion
For a binary classification task, the Binary Cross-Entropy (BCE) formula simplifies to: \[
\text{BCE} = - \big( y \ln(p) + (1 - y) \ln(1 - p) \big)
\] Calculate the BCE loss for a single data point where the true label is \(y=1\) and the model’s predicted probability is \(p=0.001\). Why does the loss become so massive compared to a wrong but less confident prediction (e.g. \(p=0.4\))?


Reveal Solution to Problem 2




Solution

If \(y = 1\), the second term \((1 - y)\) becomes zero, completely eliminating the \(\ln(1-p)\) term. The formula simplifies to: \[
\text{BCE} = -\ln(p)
\]
For \(p = 0.4\) (Wrong, but not confidently wrong): \[
\text{BCE} = -\ln(0.4) \approx 0.916 \text{ nats}
\]
For \(p = 0.001\) (Highly confident, but completely wrong): \[
\text{BCE} = -\ln(0.001) \approx 6.907 \text{ nats}
\]
The loss is over 7 times higher! The logarithmic nature of the loss function massively penalizes a model that is confidently wrong. As the probability \(p\) approaches exactly \(0\) for a true class, the loss approaches positive infinity (\(\lim_{p \to 0} -\ln(p) = \infty\)). This asymmetric explosion forces the model to heavily adjust its weights to never guarantee an incorrect prediction.

title: Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)
text:
For a probabilistic model with observed data \(\mathbf{x}\), latent variables \(\mathbf{z}\), parameters \(\boldsymbol\theta\), and a variational approximation \(q(\mathbf{z})\), the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) bounds the log-marginal likelihood \(\log p(\mathbf{x} | \boldsymbol\theta)\):
\[
\log p(\mathbf{x} | \boldsymbol\theta) \ge \mathrm{ELBO}(q, \boldsymbol\theta)
\]
It can be decomposed into a reconstruction term and a KL-divergence regularization term: \[
\mathrm{ELBO}(q, \boldsymbol\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{q(\mathbf{z})}[\log p(\mathbf{x} | \mathbf{z}, \boldsymbol\theta)] - D_{\mathrm{KL}}(q(\mathbf{z}) || p(\mathbf{z} | \boldsymbol\theta))
\]

title: Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)
text:
For a probabilistic model with observed data \(\mathbf{x}\), latent variables \(\mathbf{z}\), parameters \(\boldsymbol\theta\), and a variational approximation \(q(\mathbf{z})\), the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO) bounds the log-marginal likelihood \(\log p(\mathbf{x} | \boldsymbol\theta)\):
\[
\log p(\mathbf{x} | \boldsymbol\theta) \ge \mathrm{ELBO}(q, \boldsymbol\theta)
\]
It can be decomposed into a reconstruction term and a KL-divergence regularization term: \[
\mathrm{ELBO}(q, \boldsymbol\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{q(\mathbf{z})}[\log p(\mathbf{x} | \mathbf{z}, \boldsymbol\theta)] - D_{\mathrm{KL}}(q(\mathbf{z}) || p(\mathbf{z} | \boldsymbol\theta))
\]

title: Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)
text:
Intuition
In many complex generative models with latent variables (like Variational Autoencoders), calculating the true evidence (the probability of the data \(p(\mathbf{x})\)) is intractable because it requires integrating over all possible latent states \(\mathbf{z}\).
Instead of maximizing the true likelihood, Variational Inference maximizes a lower bound: the ELBO. By maximizing the ELBO, we implicitly push up the true log-likelihood.
The decomposition perfectly illustrates the trade-off in VAEs:

1. Reconstruction Term (\(\mathbb{E}_{q}[\log p]\))
Encourages the model to reconstruct the data well from the latent representation.


2. Regularization Term (\(-D_{\mathrm{KL}}(q || p)\))
Penalizes the approximate posterior \(q(\mathbf{z})\) for deviating from the prior \(p(\mathbf{z})\), forcing the latent space to remain structured and smooth.

title: Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)
text:
Practice Problems

1. ELBO Inequality
Prove that \(\mathrm{ELBO}(q, \boldsymbol\theta) \le \log p(\mathbf{x} | \boldsymbol\theta)\) for any variational distribution \(q(\mathbf{z})\) by using Jensen’s inequality or by showing that the difference is a KL divergence.


2. Decomposition
Show algebraically that the ELBO can be decomposed into the reconstruction term and the KL divergence term: \(\mathrm{ELBO}(q, \boldsymbol\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{q(\mathbf{z})}[\log p(\mathbf{x} | \mathbf{z}, \boldsymbol\theta)] - D_{\mathrm{KL}}(q(\mathbf{z}) || p(\mathbf{z} | \boldsymbol\theta))\).


3. VAE Regularization
In a VAE, explain how the KL divergence term acts as a regularizer. What happens to the learned representations if the KL term dominates the loss? What happens if it is too small?

title: Evaluation Metrics for Ranking and Information Retrieval
text:
Information Retrieval (IR) lies at the heart of modern search engines (Google, Bing) and recommendation systems (Netflix, Amazon). The core problem is simple: given a user query, a system retrieves thousands of documents. How do we mathematically evaluate if the system ranked the “most relevant” documents at the top?
While traditional ML classification relies on metrics like Accuracy or Cross-Entropy, Ranking systems require specialized metrics that heavily penalize errors at the top of the list (Rank 1) while forgiving errors at the bottom of the list (Rank 100).

title: Evaluation Metrics for Ranking and Information Retrieval
text:
Practice Problems

Problem 1: Mean Reciprocal Rank
Calculate the MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank) for a search engine that processes three distinct user queries, where the single correct result is found at rank \(1\), rank \(3\), and rank \(2\) respectively.

title: f-Divergence
text:
The \(f\)-Divergence (also known as the Ali-Silvey distance) is not a single formula, but a vast mathematical family that generalizes the concept of differences between probability distributions.
By simply plugging in different scalar functions \(f(t)\), you can retrieve almost all the famous divergences used in machine learning, including KL Divergence, Jensen-Shannon Divergence, and Total Variation Distance.

title: f-Divergence
text:
Practice Problems

Problem 1: Deriving KL from f-Divergence
Starting from the generalized f-divergence integral formula \(D_f(P \| Q) = \int q(x) f\left(\frac{p(x)}{q(x)}\right) dx\), prove that setting the generator function to \(f(t) = t \log t\) successfully recovers the Kullback-Leibler divergence.

title: Information Bottleneck
text:
The Information Bottleneck (IB) principle, introduced by Naftali Tishby in 1999, provides a rigorous information-theoretic framework for extracting the most relevant features from data.
Suppose you have an input variable \(X\) (e.g., an image of an animal) and a target variable \(Y\) (e.g., the label “Dog”). You want to construct an internal representation \(Z\) (e.g., the hidden activations in a neural network) that captures the essence of \(X\) needed to predict \(Y\).
The Information Bottleneck states that the optimal representation \(Z\) must satisfy two conflicting goals: 1. Prediction: \(Z\) should retain as much information as possible about the target \(Y\). 2. Compression: \(Z\) should “forget” as much irrelevant information as possible about the input \(X\).
By forcing the information to pass through a narrow “bottleneck”, the system is forced to strip away noise and memorize only the minimal sufficient statistics necessary for the task.

title: Information Bottleneck
text:
Practice Problems

Problem 1: The IB Trade-off
In the Information Bottleneck objective \(\mathcal{L} = I(X;Z) - \beta I(Z;Y)\), what happens to the learned representation \(Z\) as the Lagrange multiplier \(\beta \to 0\)? What happens when \(\beta \to \infty\)?

title: Jensen-Shannon Divergence
text:
Given two probability distributions \(P\) and \(Q\), we first define the mixture distribution \(M\) as their exact point-wise average:
\[
M = \frac{1}{2}(P + Q)
\]
The Jensen-Shannon Divergence (JSD) is then defined as the average of the KL divergences from \(P\) to \(M\), and from \(Q\) to \(M\):
\[
JSD(P \| Q) = \frac{1}{2} D_{KL}(P \| M) + \frac{1}{2} D_{KL}(Q \| M)
\]

title: Jensen-Shannon Divergence
text:
Given two probability distributions \(P\) and \(Q\), we first define the mixture distribution \(M\) as their exact point-wise average:
\[
M = \frac{1}{2}(P + Q)
\]
The Jensen-Shannon Divergence (JSD) is then defined as the average of the KL divergences from \(P\) to \(M\), and from \(Q\) to \(M\):
\[
JSD(P \| Q) = \frac{1}{2} D_{KL}(P \| M) + \frac{1}{2} D_{KL}(Q \| M)
\]

title: Jensen-Shannon Divergence
text:
2. Intuitions & Remarks

A Symmetric Fix to KL Divergence
While the Kullback-Leibler (KL) Divergence is foundational, it suffers from two major mathematical flaws that limit its utility as a true distance metric: 1. It is asymmetric (\(D_{KL}(P \| Q) \neq D_{KL}(Q \| P)\)). 2. It can explode to infinity if the distributions do not share the exact same support (i.e., if \(Q(x) = 0\) where \(P(x) > 0\)).
The Jensen-Shannon Divergence solves both of these problems by creating a smoothed, symmetric version. Instead of measuring how far \(Q\) is from \(P\) directly, it measures how far both distributions are from their perfectly balanced “middle ground” mixture \(M\).
Because \(M\) contains the probability mass of both \(P\) and \(Q\), it is impossible for \(M(x)\) to be zero when \(P(x)\) or \(Q(x)\) is greater than zero. This guarantees that the KL divergences to \(M\) never explode to infinity, providing immense mathematical stability.

title: Jensen-Shannon Divergence
text:
3. Formulas & Key Properties

Key Properties of JSD

1. Perfect Symmetry
Unlike KL divergence, JSD is perfectly symmetric. It doesn’t matter which distribution is the “reference” and which is the “approximation.” \[
JSD(P \| Q) = JSD(Q \| P)
\]


2. Strict Bounds
Unlike KL divergence which stretches from \(0\) to \(\infty\), JSD is strictly bounded between \(0\) and \(\log(2)\) (or \(1\) if using base-2 logarithms). \[
0 \le JSD(P \| Q) \le \log(2)
\] This absolute boundedness provides immense numerical stability during machine learning training, preventing exploding gradients.


3. A True Mathematical Metric
While JSD itself does not satisfy the triangle inequality, its square root actually does! The quantity \(\sqrt{JSD(P \| Q)}\) satisfies non-negativity, symmetry, identity of indiscernibles, and the triangle inequality, making it a true mathematical distance metric.

title: Jensen-Shannon Divergence
text:

The optimal discriminator forces the generator to minimize the JSD to the real data distribution.

Comparing probability distributions of amino acids to find evolutionary similarities.

Machine Learning Context: The Birth of GANs
The Jensen-Shannon Divergence is permanently etched into machine learning history due to its central role in the original Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) paper by Ian Goodfellow (2014).
In a standard GAN, a Generator network \(G\) tries to produce fake data to fool a Discriminator network \(D\), while \(D\) tries to distinguish between true data (\(P_{data}\)) and fake data (\(P_{generator}\)).
Goodfellow mathematically proved that if the discriminator \(D\) is trained to absolute optimality at any given step, the loss function of the generator precisely collapses into minimizing the Jensen-Shannon Divergence between the true data distribution and the generated distribution:
\[
\mathcal{L}_G = 2 \cdot JSD(P_{data} \| P_{generator}) - \log(4)
\]
By engaging in this minimax game, the generator implicitly forces its probability distribution to perfectly match the training data, guided by the smoothed, stable gradients of the JSD.

title: Jensen-Shannon Divergence
text:
7. Practice Problems

Problem 1: Proof of Boundedness
Prove mathematically that the Jensen-Shannon Divergence (base 2) is strictly bounded above by exactly \(1\) bit: \[
JSD(P \| Q) \le 1
\]


Reveal Solution to Problem 1




Solution

To prove the upper bound, we express JSD using Shannon Entropy \(H(\cdot)\). Let’s expand the definition:
\[
\begin{aligned}
JSD(P \| Q) &= \frac{1}{2} \left[ \sum P(x) \log_2 \frac{P(x)}{M(x)} \right] + \frac{1}{2} \left[ \sum Q(x) \log_2 \frac{Q(x)}{M(x)} \right] \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \left[ \sum P(x) \log_2 P(x) - \sum P(x) \log_2 M(x) \right] \\
&\quad + \frac{1}{2} \left[ \sum Q(x) \log_2 Q(x) - \sum Q(x) \log_2 M(x) \right]
\end{aligned}
\]
Substitute the definition of Shannon Entropy \(H(P) = -\sum P(x) \log_2 P(x)\):
\[
\begin{aligned}
JSD(P \| Q) &= \frac{1}{2} \left[ -H(P) - \sum P(x) \log_2 M(x) \right] + \frac{1}{2} \left[ -H(Q) - \sum Q(x) \log_2 M(x) \right] \\
&= -\frac{1}{2} H(P) - \frac{1}{2} H(Q) - \frac{1}{2} \sum \Big( P(x) + Q(x) \Big) \log_2 M(x)
\end{aligned}
\]
Recall that \(M(x) = \frac{P(x) + Q(x)}{2}\), meaning \(P(x) + Q(x) = 2M(x)\). Substitute this:
\[
\begin{aligned}
JSD(P \| Q) &= -\frac{1}{2} H(P) - \frac{1}{2} H(Q) - \frac{1}{2} \sum \Big( 2M(x) \Big) \log_2 M(x) \\
&= -\frac{1}{2} H(P) - \frac{1}{2} H(Q) - \sum M(x) \log_2 M(x)
\end{aligned}
\]
Notice that \(-\sum M(x) \log_2 M(x)\) is exactly the entropy of the mixture distribution \(H(M)\). Therefore:
\[
JSD(P \| Q) = H(M) - \frac{H(P) + H(Q)}{2}
\]
Because entropy \(H\) is a strictly concave function, the entropy of a mixture \(M\) is bounded above by applying Jensen’s Inequality: \[
H(M) = H\left(\frac{P+Q}{2}\right) \le \frac{1}{2} H(P) + \frac{1}{2} H(Q) + 1
\] (Note: the \(+1\) comes from the bound of a binary mixture in base 2).
Rearranging this inequality gives: \[
H(M) - \left( \frac{H(P) + H(Q)}{2} \right) \le 1
\]
Since the left side is precisely the \(JSD(P \| Q)\), we arrive at our absolute upper bound: \[
JSD(P \| Q) \le 1 \text{ bit}
\]
The equality holds if and only if \(P\) and \(Q\) have completely disjoint supports. \(\blacksquare\)





Problem 2: GAN Stabilization
Explain intuitively why this strict boundedness (\(0 \le JSD \le 1\)) makes Jensen-Shannon Divergence vastly superior to KL divergence for training early-stage Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs).


Reveal Solution to Problem 2




Solution

In the early stages of training a GAN, the Generator network is terrible. It produces random noise that looks absolutely nothing like the real data. Mathematically, this means the generated distribution \(P_{generator}\) and the real data distribution \(P_{data}\) have completely non-overlapping (disjoint) supports in the high-dimensional image space.
If you tried to minimize the Forward KL Divergence \(D_{KL}(P_{data} \| P_{generator})\), the ratio \(\frac{P_{data}(x)}{P_{generator}(x)}\) would divide by zero wherever the generator failed to place probability mass. The loss would literally explode to infinity, completely destroying the neural network’s gradients.
However, the JSD explicitly handles disjoint supports gracefully. If the distributions don’t overlap, the JSD simply saturates to its maximum bound of exactly \(\log(2)\) nats (or \(1\) bit). The loss remains a finite, well-behaved number, and the gradient flow is preserved so the Generator can continue learning to bridge the gap.

title: Markov Chain
text:
A Markov Chain is a stochastic model describing a sequence of possible events where the probability of each event depends only on the state attained in the previous event.
This strict assumption is known as the Markov Property, or “memorylessness.” It asserts that if you know the present state of a system, the entire past history of the system is completely irrelevant for predicting the future.

title: Markov Chain
text:
Practice Problems

Problem 1: The Markov Property
Define the Markov Property in your own words. Why is this memoryless assumption absolutely mathematically necessary for formulating Reinforcement Learning environments as Markov Decision Processes (MDPs)?

title: Perplexity
text:
Perplexity is a measurement of how well a probability model predicts a sample. It is an exponentiated version of entropy (or cross-entropy) that transforms the logarithmic scale of bits back into a more intuitive, linear scale of “equivalent choices.”
If a model has a perplexity of \(K\), it means the model is, on average, as “confused” or “perplexed” as if it had to guess uniformly among \(K\) equally likely options.

title: Perplexity
text:
Practice Problems

Problem 1: Language Model Perplexity
A language model assigns a uniform probability over a vocabulary of \(10,000\) words. What is its perplexity? If a better model achieves a perplexity of \(50\), what does this physically mean in terms of its predictive power?

title: Stochastic Matrices and Markov Chains
text:
While the definition of a Markov Chain is fundamentally probabilistic, executing operations and simulating transitions over time is vastly more efficient when framed using the language of Linear Algebra.
To achieve this, we encode the transition probabilities of a Markov Chain into a specific type of matrix known as a Stochastic Matrix.

title: Stochastic Matrices and Markov Chains
text:
Practice Problems

Problem 1: Stationary Distributions
Given a \(2 \times 2\) stochastic transition matrix \(P = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & 0.2 \\ 0.3 & 0.7 \end{bmatrix}\), find the stationary distribution \(\pi\) by solving the left-eigenvector equation \(\pi P = \pi\).

title: Wasserstein Distance
text:
For two probability distributions \(P\) and \(Q\), the \(1^{st}\) Wasserstein Distance \(W_1\) is defined as the infimum (minimum) expected cost of transporting mass from \(P\) to \(Q\):
\[
W_1(P, Q) = \inf_{\gamma \in \Pi(P, Q)} \mathbb{E}_{(x, y) \sim \gamma} \big[ \|x - y\| \big]
\]



\(\Pi(P, Q)\): The set of all possible “transport plans”. A joint distribution plan \(\gamma(x, y)\) dictates exactly how much mass to move from location \(x\) to location \(y\). To be a valid plan, its marginals must equal \(P\) and \(Q\).
\(\|x - y\|\): The physical distance between location \(x\) and \(y\) in the underlying metric space.
\(\mathbb{E}\): The expected cost (work) of executing the transport plan.
\(\inf\): The infimum. We are searching for the single optimal transport plan that requires the absolute least amount of work.

title: Wasserstein Distance
text:
For two probability distributions \(P\) and \(Q\), the \(1^{st}\) Wasserstein Distance \(W_1\) is defined as the infimum (minimum) expected cost of transporting mass from \(P\) to \(Q\):
\[
W_1(P, Q) = \inf_{\gamma \in \Pi(P, Q)} \mathbb{E}_{(x, y) \sim \gamma} \big[ \|x - y\| \big]
\]



\(\Pi(P, Q)\): The set of all possible “transport plans”. A joint distribution plan \(\gamma(x, y)\) dictates exactly how much mass to move from location \(x\) to location \(y\). To be a valid plan, its marginals must equal \(P\) and \(Q\).
\(\|x - y\|\): The physical distance between location \(x\) and \(y\) in the underlying metric space.
\(\mathbb{E}\): The expected cost (work) of executing the transport plan.
\(\inf\): The infimum. We are searching for the single optimal transport plan that requires the absolute least amount of work.

title: Wasserstein Distance
text:
2. Intuitions & Remarks

The Earth Mover’s Distance (EMD)
The Wasserstein Distance is intuitively known as the Earth Mover’s Distance.
Imagine probability distributions as physical piles of dirt (Earth). The Wasserstein distance asks: What is the absolute minimum amount of “work” required to physically shovel the dirt from distribution \(P\) until it perfectly matches the shape of distribution \(Q\)?
Work in physics is defined as: Amount of dirt moved \(\times\) Distance moved.
Because it explicitly measures the physical distance the mass must travel, the Wasserstein distance provides a smooth, meaningful value (and crucially, a non-zero gradient) even when the distributions are miles apart and do not overlap at all.

title: Wasserstein Distance
text:
3. Formulas & Key Properties

Overcoming the Limits of Divergences
Both the Kullback-Leibler (KL) and Jensen-Shannon (JSD) divergences are fundamentally based on the ratio of probabilities \(P(x) / Q(x)\) at exactly the same spatial location \(x\).
This mathematical foundation creates a severe problem: if the two distributions have disjoint supports (meaning they don’t overlap in space), these divergences either become infinite or saturate at a constant value.
When divergences saturate, their gradients become exactly zero. In Machine Learning, if your loss function has zero gradient, your neural network receives no signal and cannot learn.
Wasserstein distance measures the horizontal cost of moving the mass, rather than just the vertical ratio at a fixed location, completely bypassing this issue.

5. Relations to Other Fields








Field
Interpretation / Mapping
Application Example




Generative AI
Evaluating the distance between generated distributions and real data when they don’t overlap.
Wasserstein GANs (WGAN)


Operations Research
Optimal assignment of resources from supply nodes to demand nodes.
Transport/Logistics optimization


Computer Vision
Comparing color histograms or image signatures in a way that respects the distance between colors.
Image Retrieval (Rubner et al.)




Machine Learning Context: WGANs and Duality
The mathematical definition of \(W_1(P, Q)\) is beautiful, but computationally intractable to calculate directly because searching over all possible joint distributions \(\Pi(P, Q)\) is impossible for high-dimensional data like images.
In 2017, the landmark paper Wasserstein GAN solved this by applying the Kantorovich-Rubinstein Duality. The duality theorem states that instead of searching for the optimal transport plan, you can calculate the Wasserstein distance by finding a 1-Lipschitz continuous function \(f\) that maximizes the difference in expectations:
\[
W_1(P, Q) = \sup_{\|f\|_L \le 1} \left( \mathbb{E}_{x \sim P}[f(x)] - \mathbb{E}_{y \sim Q}[f(y)] \right)
\]
In a WGAN, the Discriminator (now called a “Critic”) is simply a neural network parameterized to represent \(f(x)\). To enforce the \(1\)-Lipschitz constraint (\(\|f\|_L \le 1\)), modern implementations use Gradient Penalty (WGAN-GP), ensuring the gradients of the critic respect a norm of \(1\). WGANs completely eliminate the vanishing gradient problem, resulting in vastly more stable training.

title: Wasserstein Distance
text:
7. Practice Problems

Problem 1: Disjoint Supports and Gradients
Explain mathematically why the Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence provides a zero gradient when two distributions are completely disjoint, but the Wasserstein distance provides a highly useful constant gradient.


Reveal Solution to Problem 1




Solution

Let \(P\) be the true data distribution (a spike at \(x=0\)) and \(Q_\theta\) be our parameterized model distribution (a spike at \(x=\theta\)). Suppose they are currently disjoint (\(\theta > 0\)).
For KL Divergence: The divergence is defined by the point-wise ratio at exactly the same spatial location \(x\): \[
D_{KL}(P \| Q_\theta) = \sum P(x) \log \left( \frac{P(x)}{Q_\theta(x)} \right)
\] Since \(P\) is only non-zero at \(x=0\), we only evaluate the ratio at \(x=0\). However, \(Q_\theta(0) = 0\) because \(Q_\theta\) is a spike at \(\theta\). Thus, \(D_{KL} = 1 \cdot \log\left(\frac{1}{0}\right) = \infty\).
Because it is \(\infty\) for all \(\theta > 0\), the derivative with respect to \(\theta\) is exactly \(0\). The loss landscape is a flat, infinite plateau, meaning the network has absolutely no idea which direction to move \(\theta\) to find the true data.
For Wasserstein Distance: The Earth Mover’s distance measures the physical work to transport the spike of mass from \(x=\theta\) to \(x=0\). Since the total mass is \(1\), the work is simply: \[
W_1(P, Q_\theta) = 1 \cdot \|\theta - 0\| = |\theta|
\] The gradient with respect to \(\theta\) (for \(\theta > 0\)) is exactly \(1\).
The loss landscape is a perfect V-shape \(f(\theta) = |\theta|\). This gives a beautiful, constant, non-zero gradient pointing directly toward the true data, allowing the neural network to effortlessly slide \(\theta\) down to \(0\) via gradient descent. \(\blacksquare\)
